hook: A 1688 stone of a naked boy on a bread basket, marking what was once 'the highest ground' in the City.
facts: A carved stone relief showing a naked baker's boy seated on an upturned pannier (bread basket), set into a wall just outside the Cathedral exit of St Paul's Underground station, beside the former Caffe Nero.
It is inscribed 'When you have sought the City round, Yet still this is the highest Ground' and dated 26 (some sources 27) August 1688.
Panyer Alley took its name from the boys who once sold bread from panniers here, in what was the centre of London's bakery trade.

After Farrow's Bank, which had displayed the stone as its mascot behind glass, collapsed in 1921, the relief was eventually restored to the alley in 1964 following wartime damage and 1960s redevelopment.
legend: Local tradition long held the spot to be the highest ground in the City of London, as the verse on the stone boasts, though Cornhill is in fact marginally higher.

sources.note: History of Panyer Alley and the stone, and its location beside St Paul's station
Detailed history, inscription text and 1688 date; confirms location by the Cathedral exit of St Paul's station (~51.514, -0.098)

w: A naked baker's boy carved in stone in 1688, perched on an upturned bread basket in a wall by St Paul's station — once the heart of the City's bread trade on Panyer Alley. His verse brags 'still this is the highest Ground,' though Cornhill quietly beats it. Bank mascot, Blitz survivor, reset here in 1964.

hook: A 'Roman' plunge bath that is really a Jacobean cistern, glimpsed through a window down a hidden lane off the Strand.
facts: The bath sits at 5 Strand Lane, a narrow passage off the Strand, and is owned by the National Trust; it is normally viewed through a window or by appointment.
Despite its long-held 'Roman' reputation, the structure is actually the remains of a cistern built in 1612 to feed a fountain in the gardens of old Somerset House.
The 'Roman' claim first appeared in a trade directory in 1838 and stuck, helped by a mention in Charles Dickens's 'David Copperfield', whose hero takes cold plunges there.
After neglect it was reopened as a public cold plunge bath in the 1770s; it later passed to the National Trust in the 1940s.
legend: For nearly two centuries Londoners believed it a genuine Roman survival, a myth only firmly debunked by 21st-century scholarship.

sources.note: History, 1612 cistern origin, Dickens link, National Trust ownership; gives coordinates 51.511587, -0.115601
National Trust page confirming location at 5 Strand Lane and visiting arrangements

w: A 'Roman' plunge bath down a hidden lane off the Strand — except it isn't Roman at all, but a 1612 cistern that once fed a fountain in old Somerset House's gardens. The Roman tale took hold in 1838 and stuck, helped along by David Copperfield taking his cold dips here. Peer through the window; the National Trust holds the key.

hook: A crooked 16th-century timber shop, said to be London's oldest, that borrowed its name from a Dickens novel.
facts: The building stands at 13-14 Portsmouth Street, WC2A, behind the London School of Economics, which now owns it.
It dates to around 1567 and is often called the oldest shop in central London, built partly from salvaged ship timbers.
It survived both the Great Fire of 1666 and the Blitz.

The words 'Immortalised by Charles Dickens' were painted above the door by a Victorian owner; Dickens's actual model for the shop in his 1841 novel is thought to have stood elsewhere, near the present National Portrait Gallery.
It was structurally restored in 2023.
legend: The shop trades on the claim that Dickens immortalised it in 'The Old Curiosity Shop', though the name was added after the novel was published and the connection is almost certainly spurious.

sources.note: Address 13-14 Portsmouth Street WC2A, c.1567 date, ship-timber construction, LSE ownership, Dickens-name myth
LSE history blog corroborating location and history; LSE campus is at ~51.514, -0.117

w: A crooked timber shop of around 1567 behind the LSE, built from old ship's timbers and through both the Great Fire and the Blitz — often called the oldest shop in central London. 'Immortalised by Charles Dickens,' says the sign, but a Victorian owner painted that on; his real shop stood elsewhere. A lovely lie, beautifully kept.

hook: Two club-wielding giants strike the hour on London's first minute-hand clock, watched over by the statues of King Lud.
facts: The projecting clock on the Fleet Street frontage dates from 1671 and is reputed to be the first public clock in London with a minute hand.
Two carved automata, traditionally identified as the giants Gog and Magog, turn their heads and strike the bells with clubs on the hour and quarter-hour.

In a niche on the church facade stand the statue of Queen Elizabeth I (carved 1586) and, below, three figures of ancient Britons taken from the old Ludgate, probably meant to be the legendary King Lud and his sons.
The clock was added in thanksgiving after the church survived the Great Fire of 1666, which stopped a few doors short.
legend: Gog and Magog are mythic giants said to be the guardians of the City of London.
King Lud is the legendary pre-Roman king after whom Ludgate is supposedly named.

sources.note: 1671 clock, first minute hand, Gog and Magog automata, Ludgate/King Lud statues; gives coordinates 51.514250, -0.110222
Corroborates the giants clock on Fleet Street at this location

w: Two club-wielding giants — Gog and Magog, by legend the City's guardians — strike the hours on this 1671 Fleet Street clock, said to be London's first with a minute hand. Above stands Elizabeth I, carved 1586; below, three figures of King Lud and his sons from the old Ludgate. The church dodged the Great Fire by a few doors.

hook: A deadpan shop 'purveying since 1818' tinned fear and brain jam to the living, dead and undead.
facts: Located at 159 Hoxton Street, N1, the shop sells joke 'monster' goods such as Tinned Fear, Cubed Earwax and Salt Made From Tears of Anger.
It is the public-facing front of the Ministry of Stories, a children's creative-writing and mentoring charity, and all profits go to that work.
Run largely by volunteers, it opened to humans in 2010 while claiming to have served the monster community 'since 1818'.
It was co-founded with involvement inspired by Dave Eggers's 826 Valencia / pirate-shop model in San Francisco.
legend: The shop maintains a straight-faced fiction that it has served London's vampires, werewolves, ghosts and other monsters continuously since 1818.

sources.note: Official site: address, products, link to Ministry of Stories, 'since 1818' conceit
Apple Maps coordinates 51.5314, -0.0803 for 159 Hoxton Street

w: Purveyors to London's vampires, werewolves and ghosts since 1818 — or so the straight-faced fiction goes. The shelves hold Tinned Fear, Cubed Earwax and Salt Made From Tears of Anger, and every penny funds the Ministry of Stories, a children's writing charity hidden behind the shop. It opened to humans, at least, in 2010.

hook: A 46m Italianate tower stranded in a park, the last relic of London's vast Victorian cattle market.
facts: The Portland-stone tower is the chief survivor of the Metropolitan Cattle Market, opened by Prince Albert on 15 June 1855 to replace the livestock trade at Smithfield.
It stands about 46 metres (151 ft) tall and was designed by the Corporation of London's surveyor James Bunstone Bunning as a focal point for drovers entering London.
Livestock sales ended in the 1930s and the meat market in the 1960s, leaving the tower and a few market railings.
Restored in 2016-2019, it now offers guided climbs and panoramic views over north London.

sources.note: Metropolitan Cattle Market history, 1855 opening, Bunning, 46m height; coordinates 51.5471, -0.1236
Official park page on clock-tower tours, confirming location in Caledonian Park

w: A 46-metre Italianate tower marooned in a north-London park — the last real relic of the Metropolitan Cattle Market that Prince Albert opened in 1855 to take the livestock trade off Smithfield. The drovers and beasts are long gone; the tower, restored, now lets you climb it for the view. A campanile for cattle.

hook: A tiny tombstone behind the railings off the Mall marks the dog of a Weimar ambassador — wrongly dubbed 'London's Nazi memorial'.
facts: The little gravestone stands behind the railings beside 9 Carlton House Terrace, the former German Embassy, near the top of the Duke of York Steps off the Mall.
Giro belonged to German ambassador Leopold von Hoesch and died in February 1934, reportedly electrocuted after biting through a cable in the garden.
The German inscription reads 'Giro / Ein treuer Begleiter! / London im Februar 1934 / Hoesch' — 'a faithful companion'.
The original stone was rediscovered during building works in the 1960s and reset at its present spot; a small protective cover was later added.
It is popularly mislabelled London's only 'Nazi memorial', but von Hoesch served the pre-Nazi Weimar Republic and was not a Nazi.
legend: Frequently described in guidebooks and online as 'the grave of a Nazi dog' or 'London's only Nazi memorial' — a label its history does not support.

sources.note: Ownership by von Hoesch, 1934 death, 9 Carlton House Terrace location, inscription
Confirms grave beside 9 Carlton House Terrace at the top of Duke of York Steps (~51.506, -0.131)

w: Behind the railings off the Mall, by the old German Embassy, a tiny stone marks Giro — ambassador von Hoesch's dog, electrocuted in 1934 after biting through a garden cable. 'Ein treuer Begleiter,' it reads: a faithful companion. Guidebooks love to call it London's Nazi memorial; it isn't — von Hoesch served Weimar, not Hitler.

hook: A jewel-box Victorian artists' colourman, all dark wood and jars of raw pigment, opposite the British Museum.
facts: The art-materials shop was founded in 1855 by Louis Cornelissen, of a Flemish family of lithographers, originally selling printmaking and engraving supplies and pigments.
It still stocks hand-ground raw pigments, brushes, gold leaf and traditional materials, and retains a period shopfront and fittings.
The last Cornelissen family member in the business died in 1977 and the shop briefly closed before being rescued and reopened in 1979.
In 1988 it moved to its present premises at 105 Great Russell Street in Bloomsbury, near the British Museum.

sources.note: Founded 1855, family history, 1977 closure/1979 reopening, 1988 move to 105 Great Russell Street; coordinates 51.5175, -0.1290
Official shop history page corroborating dates and Great Russell Street location

w: A jewel-box of an artists' colourman opposite the British Museum, all dark wood and apothecary jars of raw pigment, founded in 1855. It still grinds and sells the old materials — pigments, gold leaf, sable brushes — behind a period shopfront. The family line ended in 1977, the shop nearly with it, until a 1979 rescue. Pigment by the jar.
